text: In this initial view, we can see a thick, fibrous capsule adherent to the diaphragm, the posterior aspect of the left lobe of the liver, and the anterior stomach.
This is an abscess capsule.
The liver was mobilized off of the diaphragm and stomach by blunt and sharp dissection.
The left side of the fundoplication was immobilized.
Attention was then directed posteriorly, where the posterior aspect of the fundal placation was mobilized and separated from the hidal mesh.
The left interior corner of the mesh is seen here.
An abscess cavity was entered and the mesh was mobilized off of the hiatus.
The mesh is further mobilized, then completely excised from the hiatus and placed in a plastic specimen extraction bag.
Abscess wall rind is dissected off anteriorly.
A plane is created and sharp dissection is used to separate the two sides of the fundal placation.
When they have been completely divided,

The right side of the funnel location is mobilized off of the posterior esophagus and stomach on the right side, and then passed behind the esophagus and over to the left side into its normal anatomic position.
The magnitude of the injury to the distal esophagus and proximal stomach is now apparent.
The proximal stomach has nearly been amputated by the mesh.
No attempt was made to mobilize the distal esophagus
in order to protect both its structural integrity and its blood supply.
The extent of the injury is assessed and the repair has begun.
The repair suture line is started posteriorly on the right side.
A 3-0 polyglactin suture on an SH needle was utilized.
The defect was enclosed in a running fashion.
The suture was then passed posteriorly over to the left side and carried up to the level of the left cruz.
A 56 French taper bougie was then passed through the defect and directed distally into the stomach.
A second 3-0 polyglycan suture line was then started anteriorly
tied, tied to the posterior suture line, and then run from cranial to caudal, completing closure of the defect.
A final 3-0 polygalactin suture line was then used to imbricate the anterior portion of the suture line.
This repair created a collis-type gastroplasty.
A posterior 270-degree fundoplication was then carried out using zero-braided polyethylene sutures on a CT2 needle.
The two sides of the fundal placation were used to cover the gastric suture line.
A patch of viable omenum from the greater curved side of the stomach was secured anteriorly to cover the entire anterior operative field.
A 15 French fluted xylastic drain was placed over the spleen, passed from left to right behind the stomach,
and placed posteriorly into the operative field.
The free end was externalized via a leftover quadrant 5 mL port site.
A completion endoscopy was then carried out to visualize the anastomosis
Inspect the fundoplication and safely direct passage of an NG tube into the stomach.
